TOWNof LOSGATOS
Oftrce of the Town Attorney
(408) 3s4-6880
Federal Express
June 19, 1990
Ms. Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel, Irgal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 958M-0807
Re: Request for Advice on behalf of Councilmembers
Thomas J. Ferrito, Brent N. Ventura and Joanne
Benjamin.
Dear Ms. Donovan:
I have been authorized by Thomas J. Ferrito, Brent N. Ventura and Joanne
Benjamin, Councilmemberi of the Town of Ils Gatos, to seek on their behalf this
request for formal written advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b).
Councilmember Thomas J. Ferrito's mailing address is 9 Simons Way, I-os Gatos,
CA 95030. Councilmember Brent Ventura's mailing address is 131 Broadway, [.os
Gatos, CA 95030. Councilmember Joanne Benjamin's mailing address is 109
Worcester, I-os Gatos, CA 95030.
This request seeks guidance on the obligations under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1976 for Councilmembers Ferrito, Ventura
and Benjarnin. More specifically, we request your opinion regarding whether the
acceptance of travel and subsistence expenses provided by the Jewish Federation of
Greater San Jose (Federation) in connection with the annual Conference of Mayors
held in Israel requires disqualification from participation in a land use deciiion
which would have a material financial effect on the Jewish Communiw Center
(JCC). The difficulty in the analysis of this situation stems from the faci that the
T,
CIVIC CENTER. TIO EAST MAIN STREET. P.O. BOX 949. LOS GATOS, CALTFORNIA 9503I


Ms. Kathryn Donovan
June 19, 1990
Page 2
Federation has a significant relationship with the JCC, such that the JCC may appear
to be an "instrumentality" of the Federation.
STATEMENT OF FACTS
The Federation is a nonprofit, tax-exempt charitable organization. It provided air
fare and reasonable subsistence to three councilmembers to allow them to attend
the annual Conference of Mayors held in Israel in April of 1989 and 1990. The trip
was valued at $2100 and was determined by this office, in consultation with the staff
of the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC), to be a gift.
The JCC applied to the Town for an architectural and site permit and a conditional
use permit to expand its facilities by constructing a health and fitness center which
has been valued at $3,051,568. (See the attached memorandum from Scott Baker,
Director of Building Services.) The permits were denied by the Planning
Commission and the JCC is now appealing to the Town Council. The hearing on
the appeal was continued from June 18, 1990 to July 16, 1990.
Although the Federation and the JCC are incorporated separately, hold autonomous
meetings of the Board of Directors and have independent operating policies, they
also share four (4) common board members. Additionally, the Federation is housed
at the JCC facility where it pays .75 cents per square foot a month (a rent which is
considered below market) and of a $1.5 million operating budget, the Federation
provides $140,000 worth of funding to the JCC annually. The yearly operating
budget for the JCC is $1,292,000; the money received from the Federation representi
9 percent of that budget.
Enclosed you will find my agenda report to Council, which includes an excerpted
confidential opinion on the subject. It is respectfully requested that you expedite the
issuance of your advice letter since the Town council is scheduled to ad^dress this
matter on July 16, 1990.
TOWN EVALUATION
The- analysis of the applicable statutes and regulations concludes that there is, at a
minimum, the appeararce of a conflict of interest concerning the councilmembers'
participation on the JCC appeal.


Ms. Kathryn Donovan
June 19, 1990
Page 3
Acceptance of the trip to Israel was not a violation of Proposition 73 (Government
Code Section 85400) which prohibits elected officials from accepting from a single
source a gift or gifts aggregating $1,000 or more in any calendar year because the
money provided by the Federation was solely reimbursement for actual
transportation expenses and subsistence in connection with chairing and participating
in panel discussions on governmental processes.
To avoid the burden of proving that the councilmembers provided consideration of
equal or greater value for the cost of the trip ($2,100) pursuant to Government Code
82028,1recommended to the Councilmembers that the trip be considered a gift and
not income.
Government Code Section 87103(e) provides that an official who receives gifts
aggregating $250 or more must disqualiff himself or herself from pa{icipating in any
decision which would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
source of the gifts. FPPC Regulation 18702.5 provides guidance on determining
whether the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision on a nonprofit organization
which has made gifts will be considered material.
In reviewing how FPPC Regulation 18702.5(d)(2) applied to the Federation and the
JCC, although it was unclear whether or not the health and fitness center would
effect the allocation of funds from the Federation to the JCC, it was clear that the
JCC's assets would increase by over $100,000 dollars since the new building was
valued at $3,051,568.
Furthermore, it is possible the Federation would either incur or avoid additional
expenses or reduce or eliminate existing expenses in the amount of $25,000 by virtue
of its connections with the JCC. Neither the Federation or the JCC have provided
this office with any information that the Federation would not be effected by the
construction of the health and fitness building.
Based on this analysis, I have given conservative advice that the three
councilmembers consider disqualifying themselves, and that the "legally required
participation" process provided by Regulation 18701 be followed. The Council has
requested an advice letter from the FPPC to clariry the councilmembers'
participation.
Again, I would sincerely appreciate your prompt attention to this matter. If you
have further questions, please feel free to contact me. Further information about


Ms. Kathryn Donovan
June L9, L990
Page 4
the organizations involved may be obtained from Michael Papo, Executive Director,
Jewish Federation of Greater San Jose at (408) 358-3033. You may also wish to
reference FPPC advice letter to Mr. Papo A-39-636 (December 12, 1989).
Very truly yours,
MJL:JH:ymf
Mayor and Town Council
Robert F. Beyer, Interim Town Manager
Michael Papo, Executive Director, Federation of Greater San Jose

Dear !Is. Donovan:
I an writing in connection with a letter to l.Is.
Kathryn E. Donovan dated June 19, 1990 from l.{ary Jo
Levinger, Town Attorney for the Town of Los catos.
My interest in this matter is that I an the

uayors in Jerusalem, Israel as g'uests of the Jewi,sh

council menbers have asked the City Attorney to
seek guidance frorn the Fair Political Practices
Commission with reference to this natter. The Town
Attorneyr s opinion states, in part that:
I'while there nay be no legal conflict
of j-nterest, given the facts and the
circumstances surrounding the vote, the
appearance of a conflict of interest
certainly presents itself ...tr 
The law
clerk, Ms. Jennifer Hagen who prepared
the opinion, then concludes uith
reference to these councilrnernbers,
rrtherefore, I believe it would be rnost
prudent to act conservatively on this
matter, and as a unit, excuse
themselves from the vote.rr
It is rny belief, and those of the other menbers
of the Connunity center, that there is no conflict of
interest, apparent or otherwise.

In the body of the opinion -- from Section 871O3(e) -- an
official uho receives gifts aggregating $25o or nore nust
disqualify himself or herself froD participating in any decision
which rrould have a reasonably foreseeable naterial financial
affect, distinguishable from its affect on the public generally,
on the source of the gifts (itatics added). Since it is clear
that the decision rril-l- have a materlal flnancial affect on the
Community Center, the questi.on is:
Is the CorDmunity Center the source of the gifts?
The source of the gifts vas and is the Jelrish Federation
exclusively. A11 of the funds for such trip expenses !,ere provided
solely by the Federation, No portlon of the funds cane, directly
or indirectly, from the Cornrnunity Center. In fact, the Connunity
Center was not advised of the advance of the funds, did not
recommend the advance of the funds, did not direct the advance of
the funds, and in fact, had no knowtedge of the palrEent of such
funds or the naking of such trips until recently, long after the
trips were made. There is not a shadog of substance to the
allegation that the Jevish Connunity center is or was the source
of any portion of the funds provided for the trips to the
conference.
If the Federation is the source of the funds, wiII the
decision have a reasonably foreseeable affect on it?
No. The decision r{rill have no affect on the Federation.
There will be no benefit or burden to the Federation of any kind
as a consequence of the decision to be Dade upon the application
of the community center.
Section 18702.5(d) (1) provides, in part as follons! 
I'The
affect of a decision is rnaterial as to a non-profit entity lrhich
is a source of incone or qifts to the official if any of the
folloaring appliesr (d) for an entity ,hose gross annual receipts
are nore than $l nillion, but less than or equal to $10 nittion
the affect of the decision wiLl be any of the follorring: (1) the
decision uill- resuLt in an increase or decrease of the entityrs
gross annual receipts for f-iscaL year in the auount of $too,ooo or
nore. rr
There !ri11 be no change j.n the gross annual receipts of the
Jerrish Federation in this iiscal yeai or any other as a
consequence of the decision. No portion of the fees or revenues
or funds from the Jerdish Conmunity Centerrs general operating
budget. or those referrable to the operation of the exercise
facility contenplated, would enure to the benefit, directly or
indirectly of the Federation. A11 such funds are the exclusive
property of and under the exclusive donain and control of the
Community Center.
(2) The decision lrill cause the entity to incur or avoid
additional expense or to reduce or elininate existing expenses for
a fiscal year in the amount of 925,OOO or uore.
-2-


The expenses of the Jevish Federation will not be affected in
any way by the development, construction, ownership, operation or
financing of the facilities. 
No expenses rrill be reducedi no
additional expenses will be incurred. The Community Center is the
sole oirner and operator of the property and the facility and the
sole executor of its programs. A11 of the income or receipts
received in connection with the facility, its construction or
operation enure to the benefit of the Conrtrunity Center; al]. of the
expenses incurred in connection rrith the facility, its constrution
or operation rr'ill be the burden of the Cormunity Center.
(3) The decision lritt result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the entityrs assets or liabilities in the anount of
SIoo, ooo or nore.
There uill be no change of any kind in the value of the
Jelrish Federation's assets or liabilities 
as a consequence of the
construction or operation of the Community Centerts exercise
facil-ity. 
The Federation is solely a tenant under a 1ease. All
of its rights to the use and benefit of the premises, either
existing or to be constructed, are spelled out in that docuhent.
The only rights to the premises enjoyed by the Federation are the
rights to occupy a portion of the Connunity center's prenises as a
tenant and to use the library one (l) veek each Donth. They have
no other rights. I have attached a copy of that docunent for your
inspection. No portion of any change, vhether increase or
decrease. in the value of the Cornrnunity Centerts assets viII be
redound to the benefit of the Federation either directly or
indirectly.
on the other hand, it is entirely likely that the Comrnunity
Centerrs gross annuaL receipts would increase in the arnount of
$100,000 or nore,' it is equally clear that the decision lrould
cause the cotununity center to incur additional expenses in the
arnount of $25,000 or nore, and we certainly do not dispute that
the decision voul-d result in an increase in the value of lhe
Coruounity Center's assets in the anount of $IOO,OOO or more. But
since it is the Federation that was the source of the gift and no!
the Comnunity Center, these results are both apparentty and
substantively legat and proper.
Nonetheless, is the Community Center an instrulrentality of
the Federation so that in fact the prohibited changes in revenues,
expenses or assets really enures to the benefit of the federation?
At page one in the Letter of transnittal of June 1990 to your
office: 
"The difficulty in the analysis of the situatj.on stems
frorn the fact that the Federation has a significant relationship
with the JCC, such that the JCC rnay appear to be an
" instrumentality" of the Federation.rl
At page tvo in the Council Agenda Report of June 15, I99O:
...rrand that the JCC, arguably is an ( instrumentality[ of the
Federation---rl
and
-3-


At page one of the confidential opinion from Jennifer Hagan,
Law Clerk, to Mary Jo Levinger, Town Attorney, dated February I,
1990 although the tra'o organizations are incorporated separately,
hold autonomous neetings of the Board of Directors and have
independent operating policies, there is such a relationship
between them at (sic) there is the appearance that the Federation
and the,relrish ColDmunj-ty Center are not distinct corporate
structures, but that the Jewish Community Center is an
" j-nstrumentalityl of the Federation. ". . .
Neither the confidential opinion of the clerk, nor the
opinion of tor*'n council in the councj-I agenda report, nor the
Ietter of transmittal prepared by tolrn council on behalf of the
three councilmembers defj.nes " instrunentality. I'
The entj.re opinion turns on the definition of the vord and we
are left to guess a6 to its rneaning. categorically, the Jcc is
not the agent of the Federation, is not its servant, it is not its
subsidiary, nor is it its alter ego. The Centerrs actions and
policies are not directed or control-Ied in any rray by the
Federation. The Jevish cohmunity Center is not the toeans by which
the Federation operates.
The Ia!, clerk is led fron the Iappearance" that the Jewish
cohmunity center is an instrunentality of the Federation to the
conclusion there is the rrappearancer' of a Conflict of Interest.
This conclusion can only be drawn if the Jeirish conmunity Center
as such I'instrunentality[ is the real source of the funds for lhe
qift. 
This is false.
The letter from the Tolrn Attorney of June 19, 1990, copy
which is attached, does not set forth a cotnplete statenent of the
facts i.n the riatter.
From the opinion, the conclusion is bottoned on four itens,
as follows:
1. 
Ihe fact that Ithe fact that the Federation is housed at
the Jelrish community center facilities vhere it occupies
I,920 square feet of space. This office space is Ieased
on a fj-ve (5) year contract rrhere the Federation pays
$17,000 annually, or S.75 per square foot a nonth. Lee
Botnan indicates this figure reflects a nonthly rent below
fair harket, at this tihe.'r (italics added)
The facilities of the Jewish comrnunity Center consist of the
prernises fornerly known as the Berry Street Elementary School. The
grounds are approxinately 8.783 acresi the school structure itself
contains approximately 35,000 square feet of habitable space. The
Jelrish community Center purchased this site as CaLj.fornia surplus
school property in 1984. At the tine of its purchase, the school had
been used as the headquarters for the state sponsored ltedfly
eradication proqrarn throughout several counties including Sanla Clara
County, After such use, the prehises lrere easentially abandoned. At
the tihe that the Community Center purchased the property and


presented a portion of it to the Federation for Lease, use and
occupancyr it rras thoroughly dilapidated and uninhabitabte. The
Jewish Federation vas our first tenant and the Federation had to pay
over 950,000 of its own funds to make the two classroons rehted frorn
us into useable office space. The Community Center and the
Federation signed a fj.ve (5) year lease cohhencing on October 5, 1985
and when the amounts that the Federation vas| required to spend on its
own behalf are amortized over the life of the lease. it reflects an
additional $,44 plus per squre foot added to the beginning rental
rate of 5.65 or 91.09 per square foot in the beginning year. As an
experienced real estate developer of tnany years, the rental of the
pretnises in such condition at that tilre for an effective $1.00 per
square foot lras a full and fair charge. At the present tine, the
base rent is $.44 per square foot pursuant to lease structured
ihcreases for an effective of $1.19 per square foot. Other than the
free use of the Community Center Library one time each month, no
other Conmunity Center facility is a part of their tenancy. I have
attached a copy of the Lease itself to my letter for your further
guidance.
The Jerl,ish Conmunity Center also leases space to other tenants
besides the Federation. It Leases approxinately 6,775 square feet to
the religious elenentary school. That lease, as !re11 as the
Federation, vas entered into in 1985. The beginning rental vas $.72
per square feet, on a 10 nonth basis, and has since raised to $.86
per square feet, also on a l-0 rnonth basis. It is a full service
Iease as rrel,l. The Com[unity center pays for all utilities, 
cleaning
and rnaintenance.
It leases approxinately I,910 square feet to the Jerrish Famity
service. This lease was entered in 1986 at S.72 per square feet.
The rental has since raised to $.81 per square feet on a fult year
basis at present.
Rather than paying less than the other tenants, the Federation
pays nore.
2. 
"Additionatly, the Federation and the Jenish community
Center share four (4) conmon board Denbers,...'r
There are 59 directors in total for the Jevish Pederation and
the Jevish Conmunity Center. The Jewish Federation has a total of 36
directors. The Cormunity Center has a total of 23 directors. No
nenber of the Comnunity Center Board of Directors serves as an
officer of the Federation. The onLy officer of the community center
that serves as a member of the Federation Board of Di.rectors is
rnyself in my capacity as the President of the Conmunity Center.
Other than myself, no nember of the Federation Board of Directors
serves as an officer of the Conmunity Center.
3. 
rrAnd of a $1.5 million operating budget, the Federation
provides $140,O00 worth of funding to the Jelrish Community
Center annua11y. The Jewish Connunity Center operating
budget is 51,292,000. The noney received fron the
Federation reflects 98 of the Jevish Cornmunity Centerrs
operating budget. I'
-5-


For fiscal years '89-90, the Federation's operating budget iras
91,686,0OO. Of that sun, the Center recej.ved $142,00O. For fiscal
years 190-91, the Federationrs operating budget is projected to be
$2,186,354 of rhich the center has been allocated 5167,O00.
The major purpose of the Federation is to raise uoney for
various charitable beneficiaries. At the present tihe, there are 39
separate beneficiaries of the Jewish Federati-on.s allocations. The
Center is one of the!0. Three other groups or agencies, the United
Jeirish Appeal, operation Exodus, and Jewish Fanily services (Enigres)
receive larger allocations. These allocations range in size fron
close to 5oo,o00 to the UJA to 9180 dollars to Tempte Beth sholou.
In no sense is the Cohhunity Center singled out by the federatioh as
the recipient of special treatrnent or speciaL allocations. A surnmary
of the Federationrs allocations for 189-90 and '90-91 is attached to
this letter for your inspection.
The Jerdish Colulunity center is also the rec-ipient of sone 92,000
plus dollars fron the United way, and, equalLy is in no sense an
apparent instrunentality of that charitable organization either. 
The
Jewish conmunity center is a beneficiary agency of both these najor
charitable organizations, the Jevish Federation of creater san Jose
and the United Way of Santa Clara County.
4. 
'rThe Federation is a non-profit, tax exenpt charitable
orqanization. It provided airfare and reasonable
subsistence to three council nenbers to allow thern to
attend the annual Conference of Uayors held j-n Israel in
April 1989 and 1990. The trip was valued at 92,1O0 and
was deternined by this office, in consuttation vith the
staff of the Fair Political Practices comDission (fPPc),
to be a gift. "
The 9th Jerusalen conference of Mayors rras held in April of
1989. Anong others, it L,as attended by the Honorable Joanne
Benjanin, ti{ayor of Los Gatos at the tine, the Honorable Thonas J.
Ferrito, Vice l,layor of Los Gatos at the time. This Conference \das
also attended by five other municipal or county representatives fron
the United states, as lre11 as 24 other mayors and city officials
representl.ng countries from around the vorld. In fact, the Uayor of
Los catos, Joanne Benjarnin, was the Chair for the second session at
the Jerusalen Center for Planning i.n Historic Cities covering the
topic "Revitalization of the Ci-ty Center: Inpacts on Urban Quallty of
Lif e. I'
The Jewish Federation requested a letter opinion fron your
office on the 27 day of October, 1989 to nake certain that providing
such trips for these conferences would not generate confLicts of
interest, your office gave its opinion and approval on Decenber 12,
1989.
-6-


These rnayoral conferences began in 1980 as a continuing program
and have been attended by at least 80 mayors of United States cities
or county officers. They have been attended by rnayors froh small
cities, such as Nutley, New Jersey, to hajor cities, such as chicago,
Illinois. 
They have baen attended by nayors fron both coasts, fron
the south. from the north, and throughout the United States. lhe
attendance at these coirferences by the city council persons
identified in the opinion was by no means specially tailored for
their benefit, nor were they singled out for specj-al- consideratj.ons.
On the contrary, as the record reflects. they attended these meetings
as part of a continuing prograh designed to elucidate, to educate,
and to inform municipal operatives on variouE urban is6ues.
Copies of the Conference Brochure and a list of the Arerican
participants in the annual conferences are attached to this letter
for your infonnation, as vel1.
llhat, then is the Jewish Federationi vhat is the Jewish
community Center; lrhat is the refationship betveen the t!ro?
The Jelrish conmunity center is a service organization aimed at
neeting the health, recreati.onal. cultural, social and athletic needs
of children, adults, fahili.es, the elderly and individuals with
special needs through a variety of prograns. Our goal, to the extent
possible, is to run all of our progtrans on a fee for services break
even basis but because nany of our clientel cannot afford to pay the
fuII fee, the Center offers a rride range of scholarship and
subsidized programs. To nake ends meet, the JCC looks to its members
in the forn of j.ncreased fees, to the Federation and the United Way
in our annual appeal for funds and to the leadership of the JCC for
additional fund raising efforts.
The Uission of the Jewish Federation is to develop leadership,
to raise and to allocate funds needed to naintain and strengthen
Jewish identity, Jevish conununity and Jewish life, and to be an
advocate for the needs and concerns of lhe Jelrish connunity in the
south Bay, the United states, Israel and the rest of the vorld.
The relatj.onship between the Jeirish Cornmunj.ty Center and the
Federation is one of cooperation and friendship as both are 5OI(c) (3)
charitable organizations dedicated to providing services to both the
Jewish and non-Jeirish connunities in the Greater san Jose area.
We believe that the advice given to the Denbers of the Town
Council by the Tolrn Attorney regarding the appearance of a conflict
of interest is not supported by the facts. We are very concerned
that the actions taken by the Town Attorney has unfairly cast a
public shadow of doubt over our pending appearance befoie the Tolrn
Council.
we very nuch hope that your ruling viII renove this shadov Uhich
will allow us to proceed to argue the iase for our 1and use pernit on
the merits of the application.
-7-


I rrould greatly appreciate receiving a copy of your ruling,
Please feel free to calL ne should you have any questions.
Yours ,,{uly,

sections.conclusion:
there is the rrappearancer' of a Conflict of Interest.
This conclusion can only be drawn if the Jeirish conmunity Center
as such I'instrunentality[ is the real source of the funds for lhe
qift. 
This is false.
The letter from the Tolrn Attorney of June 19, 1990, copy
which is attached, does not set forth a cotnplete statenent of the
facts i.n the riatter.
From the opinion, the conclusion is bottoned on four itens,
as follows:
1. 
Ihe fact that Ithe fact that the Federation is housed at
the Jelrish community center facilities vhere it occupies
I,920 square feet of space. This office space is Ieased
on a fj-ve (5) year contract rrhere the Federation pays
$17,000 annually, or S.75 per square foot a nonth. Lee
Botnan indicates this figure reflects a nonthly rent below
fair harket, at this tihe.'r (italics added)
The facilities of the Jewish comrnunity Center consist of the
prernises fornerly known as the Berry Street Elementary School. The
grounds are approxinately 8.783 acresi the school structure itself
contains approximately 35,000 square feet of habitable space. The
Jelrish community Center purchased this site as CaLj.fornia surplus
school property in 1984. At the tine of its purchase, the school had
been used as the headquarters for the state sponsored ltedfly
eradication proqrarn throughout several counties including Sanla Clara
County, After such use, the prehises lrere easentially abandoned. At
the tihe that the Community Center purchased the property and


presented a portion of it to the Federation for Lease, use and
occupancyr it rras thoroughly dilapidated and uninhabitabte. The
Jewish Federation vas our first tenant and the Federation had to pay
over 950,000 of its own funds to make the two classroons rehted frorn
us into useable office space. The Community Center and the
Federation signed a fj.ve (5) year lease cohhencing on October 5, 1985
and when the amounts that the Federation vas| required to spend on its
own behalf are amortized over the life of the lease. it reflects an
additional $,44 plus per squre foot added to the beginning rental
rate of 5.65 or 91.09 per square foot in the beginning year. As an
experienced real estate developer of tnany years, the rental of the
pretnises in such condition at that tilre for an effective $1.00 per
square foot lras a full and fair charge. At the present tine, the
base rent is $.44 per square foot pursuant to lease structured
ihcreases for an effective of $1.19 per square foot. Other than the
free use of the Community Center Library one time each month, no
other Conmunity Center facility is a part of their tenancy. I have
attached a copy of the Lease itself to my letter for your further
guidance.
The Jerl,ish Conmunity Center also leases space to other tenants
besides the Federation. It Leases approxinately 6,775 square feet to
the religious elenentary school. That lease, as !re11 as the
Federation, vas entered into in 1985. The beginning rental vas $.72
per square feet, on a 10 nonth basis, and has since raised to $.86
per square feet, also on a l-0 rnonth basis. It is a full service
Iease as rrel,l. The Com[unity center pays for all utilities, 
cleaning
and rnaintenance.
It leases approxinately I,910 square feet to the Jerrish Famity
service. This lease was entered in 1986 at S.72 per square feet.
The rental has since raised to $.81 per square feet on a fult year
basis at present.
Rather than paying less than the other tenants, the Federation
pays nore.
2. 
"Additionatly, the Federation and the Jenish community
Center share four (4) conmon board Denbers,...'r
There are 59 directors in total for the Jevish Pederation and
the Jevish Conmunity Center. The Jewish Federation has a total of 36
directors. The Cormunity Center has a total of 23 directors. No
nenber of the Comnunity Center Board of Directors serves as an
officer of the Federation. The onLy officer of the community center
that serves as a member of the Federation Board of Di.rectors is
rnyself in my capacity as the President of the Conmunity Center.
Other than myself, no nember of the Federation Board of Directors
serves as an officer of the Conmunity Center.
3. 
rrAnd of a $1.5 million operating budget, the Federation
provides $140,O00 worth of funding to the Jelrish Community
Center annua11y. The Jewish Connunity Center operating
budget is 51,292,000. The noney received fron the
Federation reflects 98 of the Jevish Cornmunity Centerrs
operating budget. I'


For fiscal years '89-90, the Federation's operating budget iras
91,686,0OO. Of that sun, the Center recej.ved $142,00O. For fiscal
years 190-91, the Federationrs operating budget is projected to be
$2,186,354 of rhich the center has been allocated 5167,O00.
The major purpose of the Federation is to raise uoney for
various charitable beneficiaries. At the present tihe, there are 39
separate beneficiaries of the Jewish Federati-on.s allocations. The
Center is one of the!0. Three other groups or agencies, the United
Jeirish Appeal, operation Exodus, and Jewish Fanily services (Enigres)
receive larger allocations. These allocations range in size fron
close to 5oo,o00 to the UJA to 9180 dollars to Tempte Beth sholou.
In no sense is the Cohhunity Center singled out by the federatioh as
the recipient of special treatrnent or speciaL allocations. A surnmary
of the Federationrs allocations for 189-90 and '90-91 is attached to
this letter for your inspection.
The Jerdish Colulunity center is also the rec-ipient of sone 92,000
plus dollars fron the United way, and, equalLy is in no sense an
apparent instrunentality of that charitable organization either. 
The
Jewish conmunity center is a beneficiary agency of both these najor
charitable organizations, the Jevish Federation of creater san Jose
and the United Way of Santa Clara County.
4. 
'rThe Federation is a non-profit, tax exenpt charitable
orqanization. It provided airfare and reasonable
subsistence to three council nenbers to allow thern to
attend the annual Conference of Uayors held j-n Israel in
April 1989 and 1990. The trip was valued at 92,1O0 and
was deternined by this office, in consuttation vith the
staff of the Fair Political Practices comDission (fPPc),
to be a gift. "
The 9th Jerusalen conference of Mayors rras held in April of
1989. Anong others, it L,as attended by the Honorable Joanne
Benjanin, ti{ayor of Los Gatos at the tine, the Honorable Thonas J.
Ferrito, Vice l,layor of Los Gatos at the time. This Conference \das
also attended by five other municipal or county representatives fron
the United states, as lre11 as 24 other mayors and city officials
representl.ng countries from around the vorld. In fact, the Uayor of
Los catos, Joanne Benjarnin, was the Chair for the second session at
the Jerusalen Center for Planning i.n Historic Cities covering the
topic "Revitalization of the Ci-ty Center: Inpacts on Urban Quallty of
Lif e. I'
The Jewish Federation requested a letter opinion fron your
office on the 27 day of October, 1989 to nake certain that providing
such trips for these conferences would not generate confLicts of
interest, your office gave its opinion and approval on Decenber 12,
1989.


These rnayoral conferences began in 1980 as a continuing program
and have been attended by at least 80 mayors of United States cities
or county officers. They have been attended by rnayors froh small
cities, such as Nutley, New Jersey, to hajor cities, such as chicago,
Illinois. 
They have baen attended by nayors fron both coasts, fron
the south. from the north, and throughout the United States. lhe
attendance at these coirferences by the city council persons
identified in the opinion was by no means specially tailored for
their benefit, nor were they singled out for specj-al- consideratj.ons.
On the contrary, as the record reflects. they attended these meetings
as part of a continuing prograh designed to elucidate, to educate,
and to inform municipal operatives on variouE urban is6ues.
Copies of the Conference Brochure and a list of the Arerican
participants in the annual conferences are attached to this letter
for your infonnation, as vel1.
llhat, then is the Jewish Federationi vhat is the Jewish
community Center; lrhat is the refationship betveen the t!ro?
The Jelrish conmunity center is a service organization aimed at
neeting the health, recreati.onal. cultural, social and athletic needs
of children, adults, fahili.es, the elderly and individuals with
special needs through a variety of prograns. Our goal, to the extent
possible, is to run all of our progtrans on a fee for services break
even basis but because nany of our clientel cannot afford to pay the
fuII fee, the Center offers a rride range of scholarship and
subsidized programs. To nake ends meet, the JCC looks to its members
in the forn of j.ncreased fees, to the Federation and the United Way
in our annual appeal for funds and to the leadership of the JCC for
additional fund raising efforts.
The Uission of the Jewish Federation is to develop leadership,
to raise and to allocate funds needed to naintain and strengthen
Jewish identity, Jevish conununity and Jewish life, and to be an
advocate for the needs and concerns of lhe Jelrish connunity in the
south Bay, the United states, Israel and the rest of the vorld.
The relatj.onship between the Jeirish Cornmunj.ty Center and the
Federation is one of cooperation and friendship as both are 5OI(c) (3)
charitable organizations dedicated to providing services to both the
Jewish and non-Jeirish connunities in the Greater san Jose area.
We believe that the advice given to the Denbers of the Town
Council by the Tolrn Attorney regarding the appearance of a conflict
of interest is not supported by the facts. We are very concerned
that the actions taken by the Town Attorney has unfairly cast a
public shadow of doubt over our pending appearance befoie the Tolrn
Council.
we very nuch hope that your ruling viII renove this shadov Uhich
will allow us to proceed to argue the iase for our 1and use pernit on
the merits of the application.


I rrould greatly appreciate receiving a copy of your ruling,
Please feel free to calL ne should you have any questions.
Yours ,,{uly,

sections.facts:
The Federation is a nonprofit, tax-exempt charitable organization. It provided air
fare and reasonable subsistence to three councilmembers to allow them to attend
the annual Conference of Mayors held in Israel in April of 1989 and 1990. The trip
was valued at $2100 and was determined by this office, in consultation with the staff
of the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC), to be a gift.
The JCC applied to the Town for an architectural and site permit and a conditional
use permit to expand its facilities by constructing a health and fitness center which
has been valued at $3,051,568. (See the attached memorandum from Scott Baker,
Director of Building Services.) The permits were denied by the Planning
Commission and the JCC is now appealing to the Town Council. The hearing on
the appeal was continued from June 18, 1990 to July 16, 1990.
Although the Federation and the JCC are incorporated separately, hold autonomous
meetings of the Board of Directors and have independent operating policies, they
also share four (4) common board members. Additionally, the Federation is housed
at the JCC facility where it pays .75 cents per square foot a month (a rent which is
considered below market) and of a $1.5 million operating budget, the Federation
provides $140,000 worth of funding to the JCC annually. The yearly operating
budget for the JCC is $1,292,000; the money received from the Federation representi
9 percent of that budget.
Enclosed you will find my agenda report to Council, which includes an excerpted
confidential opinion on the subject. It is respectfully requested that you expedite the
issuance of your advice letter since the Town council is scheduled to ad^dress this
matter on July 16, 1990.
TOWN EVALUATION
The- analysis of the applicable statutes and regulations concludes that there is, at a
minimum, the appeararce of a conflict of interest concerning the councilmembers'
participation on the JCC appeal.


Ms. Kathryn Donovan
June 19, 1990
Acceptance of the trip to Israel was not a violation of Proposition 73 (Government
Code Section 85400) which prohibits elected officials from accepting from a single
source a gift or gifts aggregating $1,000 or more in any calendar year because the
money provided by the Federation was solely reimbursement for actual
transportation expenses and subsistence in connection with chairing and participating
in panel discussions on governmental processes.
To avoid the burden of proving that the councilmembers provided consideration of
equal or greater value for the cost of the trip ($2,100) pursuant to Government Code
82028,1recommended to the Councilmembers that the trip be considered a gift and
not income.
Government Code Section 87103(e) provides that an official who receives gifts
aggregating $250 or more must disqualiff himself or herself from pa{icipating in any
decision which would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
source of the gifts. FPPC Regulation 18702.5 provides guidance on determining
whether the reasonably foreseeable effect of a decision on a nonprofit organization
which has made gifts will be considered material.
In reviewing how FPPC Regulation 18702.5(d)(2) applied to the Federation and the
JCC, although it was unclear whether or not the health and fitness center would
effect the allocation of funds from the Federation to the JCC, it was clear that the
JCC's assets would increase by over $100,000 dollars since the new building was
valued at $3,051,568.
Furthermore, it is possible the Federation would either incur or avoid additional
expenses or reduce or eliminate existing expenses in the amount of $25,000 by virtue
of its connections with the JCC. Neither the Federation or the JCC have provided
this office with any information that the Federation would not be effected by the
construction of the health and fitness building.
Based on this analysis, I have given conservative advice that the three
councilmembers consider disqualifying themselves, and that the "legally required
participation" process provided by Regulation 18701 be followed. The Council has
requested an advice letter from the FPPC to clariry the councilmembers'
participation.
Again, I would sincerely appreciate your prompt attention to this matter. If you
have further questions, please feel free to contact me. Further information about


Ms. Kathryn Donovan
June L9, L990
the organizations involved may be obtained from Michael Papo, Executive Director,
Jewish Federation of Greater San Jose at (408) 358-3033. You may also wish to
reference FPPC advice letter to Mr. Papo A-39-636 (December 12, 1989).

embedding.first_500_words: FPPC JwZ0 tt:t Att TOWNof LOSGATOS Oftrce of the Town Attorney (408) 3s4-6880 Federal Express June 19, 1990 Ms. Kathryn E. Donovan General Counsel, Irgal Division Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 958M-0807 Re: Request for Advice on behalf of Councilmembers Thomas J. Ferrito, Brent N. Ventura and Joanne Benjamin. Dear Ms. Donovan: I have been authorized by Thomas J. Ferrito, Brent N. Ventura and Joanne Benjamin, Councilmemberi of the Town of Ils Gatos, to seek on their behalf this request for formal written advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b). Councilmember Thomas J. Ferrito's mailing address is 9 Simons Way, I-os Gatos, CA 95030. Councilmember Brent Ventura's mailing address is 131 Broadway, [.os Gatos, CA 95030. Councilmember Joanne Benjamin's mailing address is 109 Worcester, I-os Gatos, CA 95030. This request seeks guidance on the obligations under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1976 for Councilmembers Ferrito, Ventura and Benjarnin. More specifically, we request your opinion regarding whether the acceptance of travel and subsistence expenses provided by the Jewish Federation of Greater San Jose (Federation) in connection with the annual Conference of Mayors held in Israel requires disqualification from participation in a land use deciiion which would have a material financial effect on the Jewish Communiw Center (JCC). The difficulty in the analysis of this situation stems from the faci that the T, CIVIC CENTER. TIO EAST MAIN STREET. P.O. BOX 949. LOS GATOS, CALTFORNIA 9503I Ms. Kathryn Donovan June 19, 1990 Page 2 Federation has a significant relationship with the JCC, such that the JCC may appear to be an "instrumentality" of the Federation. STATEMENT OF FACTS The Federation is a nonprofit, tax-exempt charitable organization. It provided air fare and reasonable subsistence to three councilmembers to allow them to attend the annual Conference of Mayors held in Israel in April of 1989 and 1990. The trip was valued at $2100 and was determined by this office, in consultation with the staff of the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC), to be a gift. The JCC applied to the Town for an architectural and site permit and a conditional use permit to expand its facilities by constructing a health and fitness center which has been valued at $3,051,568. (See the attached memorandum from Scott Baker, Director of Building Services.) The permits were denied by the Planning Commission and the JCC is now appealing to the Town Council. The hearing on the appeal was continued from June 18, 1990 to July 16, 1990. Although the Federation and the JCC are incorporated separately, hold autonomous meetings of the Board of Directors and have independent operating policies, they also share four (4) common board members. Additionally, the Federation is housed at the JCC facility where it pays .75 cents per square foot a month (a rent which is considered below market) and of a $1.5 million operating budget, the Federation provides $140,000 worth of funding to the JCC annually.
